first_steps.title: First steps
first_steps.sub: Everything you need to know in your first month. No fluff.
first_steps.sin_title: How to get your SIN Number
first_steps.sin_lead: The Social Insurance Number (SIN) is your tax identifier in Canada. Without it you cannot work legally or access government benefits.
first_steps.sin_step1: You need your valid passport and your work or study permit (or your PR card if you are already a permanent resident).
first_steps.sin_step2: Find the nearest office at Canada.ca/service-canada. No appointment needed in most cities.
first_steps.sin_step3: The process is free and you receive your number the same day on a printed slip. Keep it in a safe place.
first_steps.sin_step4: Share it only with your employer, bank and the CRA. Never send it via unsecured email.
first_steps.sin_tip: If you arrived on a tourist visa and don't yet have a work permit, you must wait until you have one before applying for a SIN.
first_steps.sin_office_desc: Find the nearest Service Canada office by address or postal code, and check available hours and services.
first_steps.sin_office_cta: Open the official Service Canada office finder
first_steps.banca_lead: With your passport and proof of address (or an employer letter) you can open an account in your first days. Most banks have special programs for newcomers.
first_steps.banca_tip: You need your SIN before your bank appointment. Open the account within the first 15 days to simplify your first payroll deposit.
first_steps.banca_rbc_pros: Branches across Canada, solid app, good Spanish support in major cities.
first_steps.banca_rbc_cons: Monthly fees if you don't meet the minimum balance.
first_steps.banca_td_pros: Extended hours including weekends. Good options for newcomers.
first_steps.banca_td_cons: The newcomer account has a limited validity period.
first_steps.banca_scotiabank_pros: StartRight program for immigrants. Credit card with no credit history required.
first_steps.arriendo_title: Renting: avoid scams and find your place
first_steps.arriendo_lead: The rental market in {{country}} is competitive. Knowing the rules and warning signs saves you money and legal headaches.
first_steps.arriendo_scam1: Landlord "out of the country" asking for a deposit before showing you the property
first_steps.arriendo_scam2: Price well below the local market
first_steps.arriendo_scam3: Payment requested in cash or transfer without a receipt
first_steps.arriendo_scam4: No formal lease or pressure to sign quickly
first_steps.arriendo_scam5: You cannot visit the property in person before paying
first_steps.arriendo_tip: In most provinces, the landlord can ask for a maximum of 1 month deposit. Always demand a receipt and a written lease before handing over any money.
first_steps.trabajo_lead: Before job hunting, make sure your SIN is active and understand what type of work permit you have (open or closed).
first_steps.trabajo_step1_title: Adapt your CV to the Canadian format
first_steps.trabajo_step1: No photo, no age, no marital status. 1-2 pages max. Include only relevant experience and quantifiable skills.
first_steps.trabajo_step2: LinkedIn (essential), Indeed.ca, Workopolis and provincial government job portals.
first_steps.trabajo_step3: Attend chamber of commerce events, industry groups on Meetup.com and LinkedIn communities in your city.
first_steps.trabajo_step4: If it's your first local experience, look for volunteering or entry-level work to build your reference network in {{country}}.
first_steps.trabajo_tip: Many provinces have programs to recognize foreign credentials. Check your province's portal to validate your professional degree.
first_steps.visas_lead: Recognized Organizations (RO) are entities authorized by IRCC to offer Working Holiday spots within the IEC (International Experience {{country}}) program.
first_steps.visas_step1: They are private or non-profit organizations approved by the Canadian government to process IEC applications. They act as intermediaries between the applicant and IRCC.
first_steps.visas_step2: Direct IRCC spots fill up fast. ROs have reserved spots and can submit your application even when the direct pool is closed.
first_steps.visas_step3: ROs charge a service fee in addition to IRCC fees. The process includes registration, document review, invitation to apply (ITA) and permit application.
first_steps.visas_step4: Check the updated list on Canada.ca by searching "IEC Recognized Organizations". Always verify the organization appears on that official list before paying any fee.
first_steps.visas_tip: ROs do not guarantee the visa, they only facilitate the process. The final outcome depends on IRCC. Be wary of anyone promising guaranteed approval.
first_steps.visas_cta_text: Looking for professional help with your immigration process?
first_steps.coming_soon_body: We're preparing the first steps guide for migrants in {{country}}. Join our community to be the first to know.
first_steps.nz_sin_lead: The IRD Number is your tax number in New Zealand. Without it you pay up to 45% tax and many employers won't hire you. It's the first thing you need to sort out.
first_steps.nz_sin_step1: You need a NZ address to apply. Book temporary accommodation (hostel or Airbnb) for your first few days.
first_steps.nz_sin_step2_title: Apply for your IRD Number online
first_steps.nz_sin_step2: Go to ird.govt.nz, under 'Get an IRD number'. You need a valid passport and a NZ address. It usually arrives within 8–10 business days.
first_steps.nz_sin_step3: Most banks require passport + NZ address + valid visa. ANZ and ASB are the most newcomer-friendly. Some let you start the process before you arrive.
first_steps.nz_sin_step4: When starting a job, declare the right tax code for your situation (M for your main job). Errors mean paying too much or too little tax.
first_steps.nz_sin_tip: Arrive with at least NZD $8,000–12,000. The legal minimum for the WHV is NZD $4,200 but that is not enough for the first few months, especially if it takes time to find work.
first_steps.nz_sin_docs_body: Apostilled police clearance certificate · Apostilled university degrees · Driver's licence (in English or officially translated) · CV in English · Work references · Bank statements for the last 3 months · Important medical records
first_steps.nz_sin_ird_desc: You can apply for your IRD Number online through the Inland Revenue official website.
first_steps.nz_banca_lead: Opening a bank account is a top priority on arrival. Most employers pay by bank transfer and many services require a local account.
first_steps.nz_banca_anz_pros: Widely available, migrant-friendly, offers a 'New to NZ' programme
first_steps.nz_banca_anz_cons: Monthly fees if minimum conditions are not met
first_steps.nz_banca_asb_pros: Excellent mobile app, easy online opening, no monthly fee on basic account
first_steps.nz_banca_tip: ANZ and ASB let you start the account-opening process from abroad before you travel. Bring your passport, visa and NZ address to the branch.
first_steps.nz_sim_lead: Get a local SIM as soon as you land. It is essential for contacting employers and finding accommodation.
first_steps.nz_sim_one_pros: Best coverage in rural areas and regions
first_steps.nz_sim_one_cons: More expensive plans in the city
first_steps.nz_sim_spark_pros: NZ's largest network, great coverage in cities
first_steps.nz_sim_2degrees_pros: Very affordable prepay plans, ideal for WHV holders
first_steps.nz_sim_2degrees_cons: More limited coverage outside major cities
first_steps.nz_arriendo_lead: New Zealand has one of the highest costs of living in the world. Plan your finances carefully before you arrive.
first_steps.nz_cost_total: Minimum NZD $1,500–2,500 per month. Many people arrive with little money and end up desperately taking any job they can find.
first_steps.nz_city_work_body: Christchurch and Hamilton have lower competition and high labour demand
first_steps.nz_city_social_body: Auckland has the largest Latin community, but also the most competition and highest cost of living
first_steps.nz_city_nature_body: Queenstown is amazing but saturated with WHV holders. Very high costs
first_steps.nz_city_agri_body: Bay of Plenty (kiwifruit), Hawke's Bay (grapes/apples), Nelson (fruit). Plenty of work in season
first_steps.nz_arriendo_scam1: Never pay before viewing the property in person
first_steps.nz_arriendo_scam2: Be suspicious if the price is well below market rate
first_steps.nz_arriendo_scam3: Never hand over your passport as a deposit
first_steps.nz_arriendo_scam4: Do not accept cash as the only payment method
first_steps.nz_arriendo_tip: Stay in a hostel or Airbnb for the first few days while you get to know the city and look for a room. Many landlords ask for references and a deposit of 2–4 weeks' rent.
first_steps.nz_trabajo_lead: With a WHV you can legally work for any employer for up to 12 months. Here are the most accessible jobs and the platforms to find them.
first_steps.nz_job_agri_body: The most available work for WHV holders. Kiwifruit picking (Bay of Plenty), grapes and apples (Hawke's Bay), fruit (Nelson). Physical work, paid by the hour or by bin. No advanced English required.
first_steps.nz_job_hospo_body: High demand especially in Queenstown and Auckland. Requires basic-to-intermediate English. Barista and kitchen roles pay better.
first_steps.nz_job_construction_body: High demand if you have experience in carpentry, painting, electrical, welding or mechanics. These trades also open pathways to residency.
first_steps.nz_job_warehouse_body: Very common for newcomers. Shift work, physical, no strong English required. Great for earning money quickly while you improve the language.
first_steps.nz_english_title: How much English do you need?
first_steps.nz_english_basic: For manual work (agriculture, warehouse): basic or none
first_steps.nz_english_mid: For hospitality and customer service: genuine intermediate level
first_steps.nz_english_high: For professionals and residency: IELTS / PTE / OET generally required
first_steps.nz_season_ski: Ski / winter tourism: Queenstown and Wanaka (June–September)
first_steps.nz_wage_body: New Zealand updates the minimum wage regularly. Check the current rate on Employment New Zealand. Never accept pay below the minimum, illegal cash-in-hand work, or any arrangement where you pay for a job.
first_steps.nz_culture_good: They value: punctuality, honesty, a calm attitude, independence and direct communication
first_steps.nz_culture_bad: They do not tolerate: lateness, CV exaggeration, conflict-seeking or lack of initiative
first_steps.nz_taxes_body: Most WHV holders pay between 17.5% and 30% income tax. Without an IRD Number you pay up to 45%. At the end of the tax year (31 March) you can claim a refund if you overpaid.
first_steps.nz_driving_tip: You can use your home-country licence for 12 months. It must be in English or officially translated. In NZ you drive on the left.
first_steps.nz_trabajo_tip: Avoid saturated cities (Auckland, Queenstown) if you arrive without a job lined up. Christchurch and Hamilton offer more opportunities and a lower cost of living.
first_steps.nz_visas_title: Visas for Living in New Zealand
first_steps.nz_visas_lead: There are several migration pathways. The WHV is the most common for young people, but it is not the only option.
first_steps.nz_whv_body: The Chile–NZ WHV lets you live for up to 12 months, work legally, study for up to 6 months and enter and leave freely.
first_steps.nz_whv_req3: No dependent children travelling with you
first_steps.nz_whv_req5: Onward ticket or funds to purchase one
first_steps.nz_student_body: Widely used for learning English or completing a diploma. Allows up to 20 hours of work per week. Some courses lead to a Post Study Work Visa and can be a pathway to residency.
first_steps.nz_aewv_body: You need a genuine job offer from an accredited employer. This is the most direct route to residency if you have an in-demand profession (nursing, IT, construction, engineering, electrical, mechanics, veterinary, teaching, welding).
first_steps.nz_residence_body: Most common routes: Skilled Migrant Category · Green List · Work to Residence · Partner of a citizen/resident · Care Workforce sector · Construction / Transport
first_steps.nz_strategy_body: The WHV alone does NOT automatically lead to residency. The typical route is: WHV → skilled employment → Accredited Employer Work Visa → residency. If you have an in-demand profession, consider going directly for a work visa. If your English is weak, invest in improving it first.
first_steps.nz_reality_body: New Zealand does need migrants, but it is more demanding than before. Authorities now scrutinise genuine job roles, salaries, experience, English level and potential migration fraud more carefully.
first_steps.nz_visas_tip: Never pay for work, never hand over your passport as a deposit and never accept informal work arrangements. Doing so can destroy your future visa options.
first_steps.nz_health_body: New Zealand has a public health system, but WHV holders generally do not have full coverage. Comprehensive medical insurance is mandatory for your entire stay.
first_steps.whv_venezuela_ca: Venezuela does not have an IEC agreement with Canada. Options include Work Permit, Student Visa, or Humanitarian Visa.
first_steps.whv_venezuela_nz: Venezuela does not participate in the NZ Working Holiday program. Explore Work Visa or Student Visa options.
first_steps.whv_venezuela_au: Venezuela does not have a Working Holiday agreement with Australia. Explore Student Visa, Employer Sponsored Visa, or humanitarian options.
first_steps.whv_check_nz_co: Colombia does not have a standard WHV with New Zealand. Check the official Immigration NZ website for any new agreements.
first_steps.au_tfn_lead: The TFN is your Australian tax identification number. Without it you'll pay tax at the highest rate. Applying is free and handled by the ATO. Apply as soon as you arrive.
first_steps.au_tfn_step1: Make sure your Work and Holiday Visa (Subclass 462) is active. The TFN can only be applied for once you're in Australia.
first_steps.au_tfn_step2: Go to the ATO website and complete the online application. You'll need your passport and Visa Grant Number.
first_steps.au_tfn_step3: It arrives by post within 28 business days. If you have a myGov account linked to the ATO, you can view it online sooner.
first_steps.au_tfn_step4: Your TFN is confidential. Share it only with your employer, bank, and the ATO. Never send it by email or on social media.
first_steps.au_tfn_tip: Apply for the TFN as soon as you arrive. It's 100% free — if any site charges you, it's a scam.
first_steps.au_tfn_docs_body: Valid passport · Visa Grant Number (in your approval email) · Australian address
first_steps.au_tfn_ato_desc: Apply directly on the official ATO website. Free and fully online.
first_steps.au_super_box_body: Your employer must deposit 11% of your gross salary into a superannuation fund. When you leave Australia you can claim it back via the DASP process. Popular funds for WHV holders: AustralianSuper, Hostplus, REST.
first_steps.au_banca_lead: Most Australian banks have newcomer programs and let you open an account before you arrive. Bring your passport and visa details.
first_steps.au_banca_commbank_pros: Most used app in Australia, simplified process for new residents, excellent digital service
first_steps.au_banca_commbank_cons: Monthly fee if you don't meet the minimum deposit requirement
first_steps.au_banca_anz_pros: No fee for the first 12 months, easy online opening, good customer support
first_steps.au_banca_anz_cons: Customer service response times can be slow
first_steps.au_banca_nab_pros: No fees with basic conditions, good for temporary workers
first_steps.au_banca_nab_cons: App less intuitive than CommBank or ANZ
first_steps.au_banca_westpac_pros: "New to Australia" program, wide branch and ATM network
first_steps.au_banca_tip: Tip: CommBank and ANZ let you open an account before arriving with just your passport number. Arrive in Australia with a debit card ready.
first_steps.au_sim_lead: Get a prepaid SIM at the airport or at any Coles, Woolworths, or electronics store. Choose based on where you plan to work.
first_steps.au_sim_telstra_pros: Best coverage in rural and remote areas — ideal for farm work
first_steps.au_sim_telstra_cons: The most expensive of the three main options
first_steps.au_sim_optus_pros: Good price-coverage balance, wide network in cities and suburbs
first_steps.au_sim_optus_cons: Rural coverage more limited than Telstra
first_steps.au_sim_vodafone_pros: Good city prices, convenient short-term plans
first_steps.au_sim_vodafone_cons: Coverage outside cities can be patchy
first_steps.au_arriendo_lead: Housing costs vary widely by city. Sydney and Melbourne are the most expensive but have the most job opportunities. Brisbane and Perth offer better value.
first_steps.au_cost_room: Shared room: AUD $200–350/week in Sydney/Melbourne · AUD $150–250 in Brisbane/Perth
first_steps.au_cost_food: Food: ~AUD $80–150/week cooking at home (Coles or Woolworths)
first_steps.au_cost_total: Estimated minimum budget: AUD $1,200–1,800/month in big cities · AUD $900–1,400 in smaller cities
first_steps.au_city_sydney_body: The most cosmopolitan and expensive. Great job market in hospitality, IT, and construction. Good for your first landing if you have savings.
first_steps.au_city_melbourne_body: Large Latin American community, vibrant cultural scene. Great for hospitality and services. Weather changes dramatically throughout the day.
first_steps.au_city_brisbane_body: Warm climate, more affordable living, growing job demand. 2032 Olympics host. Great option to start out.
first_steps.au_city_perth_body: Great for mining or WA agriculture work. Geographically isolated but with good wages. Very useful for completing the 88-day regional work requirement.
first_steps.au_arriendo_scam1: Requests for deposit without viewing the property in person or by video call
first_steps.au_arriendo_scam2: Prices far below local market rates
first_steps.au_arriendo_scam4: Duplicate listings using real property photos from Realestate.com.au
first_steps.au_arriendo_tip: Flatmates.com.au is the best option for sharing with other migrants and cutting costs. Always inspect the property before paying any deposit.
first_steps.au_trabajo_lead: Australia's minimum wage is AUD $24.10/hour (2024–25). Opportunities are varied: farm work, hospitality, construction, and more. Regional work is also key to extending your visa.
first_steps.au_job_agri_body: 88 days of regional work are needed to extend to a 2nd and 3rd year. Fruit, vegetable, and vineyard harvesting. Platforms: Harvest Trail, Gumtree, labour hire agencies.
first_steps.au_job_hospo_body: Cafés, restaurants, and bars: high demand for WHV holders. No experience needed to get started. Very common in Melbourne, Sydney, and tourist areas.
first_steps.au_job_construction_body: Excellent wages and high demand. Requires a White Card (safety certificate), obtained online in ~1 day for ~AUD $35.
first_steps.au_job_warehouse_body: Well-paid physical work with steady demand. Forklift licence is a plus. Many options through labour hire agencies.
first_steps.au_super_tip: 💰 Superannuation: your employer contributes 11% of your salary to a retirement fund. When you leave Australia you can claim it back via the DASP process.
first_steps.au_farm_body: The official Australian government Harvest Trail site lists approved farms and employers by region. It's the safest starting point for finding regional work that counts toward your visa extension.
first_steps.au_trabajo_tip: Get your White Card (construction safety cert) as soon as you arrive — it costs ~AUD $35 online and opens many doors in construction and civil works.
first_steps.au_taxes_body: The Australian financial year runs July to June. If you're a tax resident (183+ days in AU), you have a tax-free threshold. Otherwise you pay from the first dollar at a higher rate. Consider a tax agent for your annual return.
first_steps.au_visas_lead: The Work and Holiday Visa (Subclass 462) is the entry point for most Latin Americans. Chile, Argentina, Colombia, and Mexico have active agreements with Australia.
first_steps.au_whv_body: Allows you to live, travel, and work in Australia for up to 12 months. Can be extended to a 2nd and 3rd year by completing regional work. Can only be applied for once.
first_steps.au_whv_req1: Age: 18–30 years at time of application
first_steps.au_whv_req2: Passport from a country with a bilateral agreement with Australia
first_steps.au_whv_req3: Functional English (no certificate required, but assessed)
first_steps.au_whv_req5: Letter of support from your government (varies by country)
first_steps.au_whv_req6: No relevant criminal record · Health insurance recommended
first_steps.au_whv_extension_body: 2nd year: complete 88 days (3 months) of specified regional work (farm work, construction, mining, childcare in rural areas, etc.). 3rd year: an additional 88 days in natural disaster areas or northern Australia.
first_steps.au_student_body: Allows study and work up to 48 hours per fortnight. Good option to improve English and extend your stay. No regional work requirement to renew.
first_steps.au_skilled_body: For professionals with in-demand skills. Requires credential assessment and a points test. The process takes time but can lead to permanent residency.
first_steps.au_residence_body: Skilled Independent (189) · Employer Nomination (186) · Partner with citizen/resident · Regional visas (491/494)
first_steps.au_health_body: Australia has no reciprocal healthcare with Latin American countries. You need private health insurance. Popular options for WHV holders: Bupa, NIB, Allianz Travel. Approx. AUD $50–80/month.
first_steps.au_visas_tip: Apply for Subclass 462 from your home country before travelling. Processing can be immediate or take weeks depending on the time of year. Apply well in advance.
first_steps.au_strategy_body: Year 1: WHV + farm work to extend · Year 2: stabilise work and improve English · Year 3+: assess permanent residency or plan your next destination.

hero.eyebrow: Latin community connecting with local talent
hero.title: Find the services you
hero.subtitle: SoyManada brings together professionals and businesses that work with the Spanish-speaking migrant community. Explore, compare options, and connect directly.

stats.ticker_1: ✅ Every provider goes through manual verification
stats.ticker_3: 🐾 Your review helps the next migrant

community.title: Real stories from the Manada in Canada
community.sub: Not statistics. People just like you who already took the leap.
community.cristobal_quote: I found work in demolition in less than two weeks. The hardest part was finding housing and managing payments when I arrived. La Manada helped me with all the document information before leaving Chile.
community.valeria_quote: I arrived from El Bosque with very basic English and a lot of fear. La Manada told me to just go for it — and they were right. You learn it much faster here than in any course.
community.nandy_quote: I found childcare work before leaving Chile. I arrived with everything organized thanks to La Manada: travel insurance, orientation, logistics. French was the hardest part, but a bilingual family helped me adapt fast.

value_props.title: The information you need,
value_props.lead: Verified directory, active community, and a WhatsApp group where over 500 Spanish-speaking migrants already support each other.
value_props.item1_title: By the community, for the community
value_props.item1_body: Every provider was recommended or validated by real Manada members. We don't list strangers.
value_props.item2_body: One click on WhatsApp and you're talking directly to someone who can help. No forms, no waiting.
value_props.item3_body: Directory, first steps guides, remittance comparator and active community. One place for your arrival in Canada.

how_it_works.title: How does it work?
how_it_works.lead: From "I don't know who to call" to talking with someone you trust in three steps.
how_it_works.step1_body: Insurance, immigration, work, housing, languages. Everything organized, no noise.
how_it_works.step2_body: Name, service, countries, languages, and whether they're verified by the Manada community.
how_it_works.step3_body: One click opens WhatsApp. No forms. No waiting. Talk to them today.

cta_section.title: Reach those who
cta_section.body: Thousands of migrants search for trusted services every week. Appearing on SoyManada means appearing in their community's directory.
cta_section.perk2: Direct contact via WhatsApp and Instagram
cta_section.cta: I want to appear on SoyManada

trust_badge.title: What does
trust_badge.lead: It's not a legal certificate. It's something more valuable: validation from real people who have already worked with this provider and recommend them.
trust_badge.disclaimer: ⚠️ SoyManada is a reference directory. It does not provide legal, immigration, or financial services. Always evaluate your provider before hiring.
trust_badge.pillar1_body: Every provider was suggested or endorsed by real Manada members. We don't list strangers.
trust_badge.pillar2_body: We validate real presence: active profile, community history, and direct contact method.
trust_badge.pillar3_body: Being on SoyManada can't be bought. It's earned through trust. The badge is merit-based, not money-based.

provider_card.gate_text: Join for free — see contacts, leave your paw print 🐾 and help the community choose better.

registro_proveedores.title: Your next client
registro_proveedores.subtitle: SoyManada connects trusted providers with thousands of migrants who need real help. No expensive ads, no algorithms. Just direct trust.
registro_proveedores.cta_primary: I want to appear on SoyManada
registro_proveedores.cta_note: We review all applications · Response within 48 hours
registro_proveedores.benefit1_body: Thousands of migrants search for trusted services every week. Your profile will be right where they look.
registro_proveedores.benefit2_body: Users contact you via WhatsApp or Instagram. No middlemen, no commissions.
registro_proveedores.benefit3_body: If you meet the criteria, you receive the "Verified by Manada" badge. Worth more than any ad.
registro_proveedores.benefit4_body: Appearing on SoyManada has no cost. Community trust is the only requirement.
registro_proveedores.step1_body: Complete the form with your name, service, countries, and contact info.
registro_proveedores.step2_body: Our team validates your information and community presence.
registro_proveedores.step3_body: Your profile goes live and you start receiving direct contacts.
registro_proveedores.final_cta_body: It's free, it's simple, and it reaches people who need what you offer.

pricing_page.title: Choose how you
pricing_page.subtitle: From basic visibility to priority positioning. No commitment. Cancel anytime.
pricing_page.bronze_desc: Take your first steps with the Manada. Explore the platform, learn how it works, and start showing up without friction.
pricing_page.silver_desc: Start your journey within the Manada. Explore, learn, and take your first steps with support.
pricing_page.gold_desc: Unlock the full strength of the Manada. Connect deeper, engage fully, and accelerate your reach among equally committed migrants.
pricing_page.feature_verified_criteria: Verified badge (if you meet the criteria)
pricing_page.note: Prices in USD · Chile: Wonderer free · Cub $4,990 CLP/mo · Wolf $9,990 CLP/mo · No commitment
pricing_page.faq1_q: Can I change plans at any time?
pricing_page.faq1_a: Yes. You can upgrade or downgrade whenever you want. Changes take effect in the next billing cycle.
pricing_page.faq2_q: How does the verified badge work on Bronze?
pricing_page.faq2_a: On Bronze, the badge is granted manually if you meet the community criteria. On Silver and Gold, the verification process is prioritized and guaranteed.
pricing_page.faq3_q: Do you accept payments from Chile?
pricing_page.faq3_a: Yes. We accept international cards and local transfers for both Canada and Chile.
pricing_page.faq4_a: No. All plans are month-to-month. Cancel anytime from your profile, no questions asked.
pricing_page.bottom_body: Thousands of Spanish-speaking migrants search for trusted services every week.

founder.quote: Migrating alone is hard. Doing it with the right pack changes everything.
founder.bio: I'm a migrant. I know what it costs to arrive in a new country without knowing anyone — not knowing who to trust with your visa, your money, or your mental health. SoyManada was born from that frustration, so the next migrant doesn't have to search alone.
founder.name: Francisco Aleuy

provider.og_description: {{service}} — Verified provider on SoyManada, the trusted directory for the Hispanic migrant community.
provider.og_description_empty: Verified provider on SoyManada, the trusted directory for the Hispanic migrant community.

auth_banner.headline: Join La Manada — it's free 🐾
auth_banner.copy: See real contacts from community-verified providers and help protect other migrants from scams.

suggest_provider.title: Suggest a provider

manadaStories.title: La Manada speaks
manadaStories.subtitle: Migrants who have already walked the path you're thinking about taking.

daniela.name: Daniela
daniela.quote: We're a family who moved from Santiago to Toronto three years ago. One of our biggest challenges at the beginning was finding a place to rent — it wasn't easy at all — but we made it happen. Now we're really happy, settled, and closer than ever as a family 💓
daniela.alt: Daniela and her family, migrants from Santiago living in Toronto

hugo.name: Hugo
hugo.quote: I came from Santiago and arrived in Calgary where I couldn't find work in the middle of winter, but Banff welcomed me with open arms. The hardest part was the language and finding work. La Manada helped me with questions about PR and everyday things — like filing taxes for the first time.
hugo.alt: Hugo in Banff, Alberta, migrant from Santiago

claudia.name: Claudia
claudia.quote: I arrived in May 2025 from Coquimbo to a ski resort, with basic English but a real desire to improve it. La Manada encourages you to trust yourself, step outside your comfort zone, and take that leap of faith. I decided to stay the whole WHV year in the same job, made great friends, and learned to ski.
claudia.alt: Claudia at Sun Peaks, BC, during her ski resort work season

amelia.name: Amelia
amelia.quote: We're a Chilean family from Iquique who arrived in Nova Scotia in 2025 in search of a better future for our children. With effort and dedication we've been building our new life. I work as an emergency nurse, my husband in construction. Like many immigrant families, our path is marked by resilience, sacrifice, and love.
amelia.alt: Amelia and her Chilean family in Nova Scotia, Canada

cristobal.name: Cristóbal
cristobal.quote: I found work in demolition in less than two weeks. The hardest part was finding housing and managing payments when I arrived. La Manada helped me with all the information before leaving Chile.

valeria.name: Valeria
valeria.quote: I arrived with very basic English and a lot of fear. La Manada told me to just go for it — and they were right. You learn it much faster here than in any course.

nandy.name: Nandy
nandy.quote: I found childcare work before leaving Chile. I arrived with everything organized: travel insurance, orientation, logistics. French was the hardest part, but a bilingual family helped me adapt.

manual.title: ❓ Provider Manual
manual.subtitle: Everything you need to know to get the most out of SoyManada.

perfil-completar.title: How do I complete my profile?
perfil-completar.content:
Your profile is the first thing a migrant sees when they find you in the directory. A complete profile builds more trust and generates more contacts.

Essential fields:
• **Name / Brand** — your real name or your brand name. E.g.: "María González" or "Migrando Contigo". It cannot be generic (e.g.: "Insurance", "Translations").
• **Main service** — a short phrase describing what you do. E.g.: "Immigration consulting to Canada".
• **Description** — tell the migrant who you are and how you can help them. Minimum 3 sentences.
• **Countries where you operate** — separated by commas. E.g.: Canada, Chile.
• **Languages** — the languages in which you can provide service.

⚠️ If the name is too generic, the **Save changes** button is automatically disabled until you correct it.

Remember to click **Save changes** at the bottom of the section.

perfil-foto.title: How to upload your profile photo
perfil-foto.content:
A real photo increases migrants' trust in you and sets your profile apart from others.

Steps:
1. Go to the **My Profile** tab.
2. Click on the circle with your initial (or on your current photo).
3. Select a JPG, PNG, or WebP image of **maximum 2 MB**.
4. The photo is saved automatically — you don't need to click Save.

Recommendation: use a real photo of yourself or your brand logo with a light background.

perfil-pago-externo.title: Adding an external payment link
perfil-pago-externo.content:
You can add a payment link that will appear as a button on your public profile.

Accepts any payment platform:
• MercadoPago: https://mpago.la/your-link
• Wise: https://wise.com/pay/your-name
• PayPal: https://paypal.me/your-name
• Any other platform with a direct URL

Steps:
1. Go to the **My Profile** tab.
2. In the "Contact and payments" section, paste your link in the "Payment link" field.
3. The link must start with https://
4. Click **Save changes**.

The button will appear on your profile immediately.

perfil-contrasena.title: Changing your password
perfil-contrasena.content:
If you signed in with email and password (not with Google), you can change it directly from your profile.

Steps:
1. Go to the **My Profile** tab.
2. Scroll down to the "Password" section (at the bottom of the page).
3. Click **Change**.
4. Enter your new password (minimum 6 characters) and confirm it.
5. Click **Save password**.

If you signed in with Google, this section does not appear — your password is managed by Google directly.

herramientas-whatsapp.title: Activating your WhatsApp on your profile
herramientas-whatsapp.content:
By default, your number is not visible on your public profile. You can enable it manually.

Steps:
1. Go to **Tools**.
2. In the "WhatsApp" section, turn on the toggle.
3. It saves automatically.

When enabled, migrants see a WhatsApp button on your profile and can contact you directly.

⚠️ Make sure you have entered your number in **My Profile → WhatsApp** before enabling this option.

herramientas-agenda.title: Setting up your appointment calendar
herramientas-agenda.content:
The calendar lets you receive bookings directly from your public profile.

You have two options:

**Option A — SoyManada's built-in calendar (recommended)**
1. Go to **Tools → Appointment Calendar**.
2. Use the availability editor to mark your free time slots.
3. Migrants will be able to book directly from your profile.

**Option B — External link (Calendly, Cal.com, etc.)**
1. Paste your Calendly or Cal.com link in the "Calendar link" field.
2. Save. Your profile will display that link as an additional button.

You can use both at the same time.

**Video call platform**
In the same section you configure where the call will take place:
• A SoyManada automatic room (Jitsi) is generated upon confirming each booking.
• You can also paste a Zoom, Google Meet, Teams, or WhatsApp link. That link appears in the migrant's confirmation email.

herramientas-stripe.title: Setting up secure payments with Stripe
herramientas-stripe.content:
Connect your bank account through Stripe to receive card payments directly from your profile.

Steps:
1. Go to **Tools → Secure payments with Stripe**.
2. Click **Connect my Stripe account**.
3. You will be redirected to the Stripe portal to verify your identity and bank account.
4. Once completed, you are automatically returned to SoyManada.

Possible statuses:
• **Not connected** — you haven't started the process yet.
• **Incomplete onboarding** — you started but didn't finish. Click "Complete verification".
• **Pending requirements** — Stripe needs more information. Go to "View pending requirements".
• **Active** — your account is verified and you can receive payments.

When active, the Stripe payment button appears on your public profile.

herramientas-email.title: Inquiry forwarding email
herramientas-email.content:
Every new message in your SoyManada inbox is also forwarded to your personal email.

Steps:
1. Go to **Tools → Advanced tools**.
2. Enter your email in the "Inquiry forwarding email" field.
3. Click **Save**.

herramientas-respuestas.title: Predefined replies
herramientas-respuestas.content:
Define texts you can quickly reuse when replying to messages in your inbox.

How to set them up:
1. Go to **Tools → Advanced tools**.
2. Click **Add question and answer**.
3. Write the frequently asked question and the corresponding answer.
4. You can add multiple pairs. Click **Save**.

Examples of useful replies:
• "My first consultation is free and lasts 30 minutes."
• "I operate Monday to Friday, 9 am to 6 pm (Santiago time)."
• "You can book directly from my profile."

resenas-responder.title: How to respond to client reviews
resenas-responder.content:
When a migrant leaves a review on your profile, you can reply to it publicly from the Reviews tab.

Steps:
1. Go to the **Reviews** tab.
2. You will see your clients' reviews with author, rating, and text.
3. Click on the text area below the review, write your reply, and click **Reply**.
4. Your reply will appear on your public profile below that review.

To edit an already published reply, click **Edit reply**.

Public replies demonstrate professionalism and build trust with new migrants who visit your profile.

resenas-como-llegan.title: How do I receive reviews from my clients?
resenas-como-llegan.content:
Reviews are generated automatically after a completed appointment.

Flow:
1. A migrant books and you confirm the appointment.
2. Once the appointment takes place, you mark the booking as **Completed** in the Bookings tab.
3. SoyManada automatically sends an email to the migrant inviting them to leave a review.
4. If the migrant leaves a review, it appears on your public profile and in your Reviews tab.

You cannot request or delete reviews manually — the process is automatic to guarantee authenticity.

reservas-gestionar.title: How to manage pending bookings
reservas-gestionar.content:
When a migrant books an appointment with you, it appears in the **Bookings** tab with a "Pending" status.

Available actions:
• **Confirm** — accept the booking. The migrant receives an email with the details and the link to the video call room.
• **Reject** — cancel the booking. The migrant is notified automatically.

Once confirmed, the booking appears under "Confirmed". When the appointment takes place, mark it as **Completed** to enable the review request to the migrant.

reservas-confirmacion.title: What the migrant receives when an appointment is confirmed
reservas-confirmacion.content:
When you confirm a booking, SoyManada automatically sends emails to both parties.

**To the migrant:**
- Date and time of the appointment
- Notes if any
- Button to enter the video call room

**To you (provider):**
- Appointment summary
- Link to the room

The video call room is generated automatically upon confirmation and is accessible from the **Bookings** tab in your account and the migrant's.

You don't need to do anything additional — the system takes care of everything.

recomendaciones-que-son.title: What are provider-to-provider recommendations?
recomendaciones-que-son.content:
Recommendations are endorsements that a verified provider can give to another. When someone recommends you, a badge appears on your directory card that says "Recommended by [name]".

This increases migrants' trust when they find you and sets your profile apart from others.

Each provider can issue up to **5 active recommendations** in total. You can withdraw them at any time.

recomendaciones-como-dar.title: How to recommend another provider
recomendaciones-como-dar.content:
You can recommend providers already on SoyManada or suggest someone new to join.

**Recommend an existing provider:**
1. Go to the **Recommendations** tab.
2. Click **Recommend someone**.
3. In the "Search existing provider" tab, type the name.
4. Select them from the list.
5. Write your recommendation (minimum 10 characters, maximum 120).
6. Click **Confirm recommendation**.

**Recommend someone new:**
1. In the same modal, go to the "Recommend someone new" tab.
2. Fill in: name, category, service offered (required) and contact (optional).
3. Write your recommendation and confirm.
4. The person will be pending review by the Manada team before appearing in the directory.

recomendaciones-retirar.title: How to withdraw a recommendation
recomendaciones-retirar.content:
You can withdraw any recommendation you have given at any time.

Steps:
1. Go to the **Recommendations** tab.
2. You will see the list of all your active recommendations.
3. Click the ✕ icon on the right side of the one you want to withdraw.
4. Confirm in the dialog.

The recommendation is deactivated immediately and the badge disappears from the other provider's profile. The record is kept internally but marked as inactive.
